concepts.title: Attention Pooling
concepts.summary: Attention pooling forms a linear combination of stored values.

provenance.quotedText: As such, the attention over generates a linear combination of values contained in the database.

steps.text: Attention pooling creates a weighted sum, called a linear combination, of values stored in a database.

citation.quotedText: As such, the attention over generates a linear combination of values contained in the database.

analogies.cooking: Imagine blending several stored ingredients, using more of some and less of others to create one combined mixture.
analogies.sports: Think of combining player statistics, giving each player a different importance, to produce one team score.
analogies.music: Imagine mixing recorded tracks at different volumes so their weighted contributions form one final sound.
analogies.video-games: Like combining several stored character abilities, assigning each a strength, to produce one overall effect.
analogies.cars: Imagine blending inputs from several stored sensor readings, giving each a different influence on one combined result.
analogies.gardening: Think of mixing stored soil components in chosen proportions to create one final blend.

steps.text: If exactly one weight is 1 and every other weight is 0, attention works like a traditional database query.

citation.quotedText: Exactly one of the weights is 1, while all others are 0. This is akin to a traditional database query.

analogies.cooking: Imagine choosing one pantry ingredient completely while ignoring all others, like retrieving one exact requested item.
analogies.sports: Like selecting one player's record with full focus while giving every other player's record no influence.
analogies.music: Imagine soloing one recorded track and muting all the others, so only the selected track remains.
analogies.video-games: Think of selecting one inventory item while every other item stays inactive, like an exact lookup.
analogies.cars: Imagine selecting one exact stored vehicle record while ignoring every other record in the system.
analogies.gardening: Like choosing one specific seed packet from storage while leaving all other packets untouched.

steps.text: If all weights are equal, attention averages every value in the database; in deep learning, this is called average pooling.

citation.quotedText: All weights are equal, i.e., for all i. This amounts to averaging across the entire database, also called average pooling in deep learning.

analogies.cooking: Imagine using equal spoonfuls of every stored ingredient, making the result reflect their average contribution.
analogies.sports: Think of giving every player's statistic equal importance, then averaging them into one team-wide result.
analogies.music: Imagine mixing every track at the same volume, so each contributes equally to the final sound.
analogies.video-games: Like averaging every character's score with equal influence to create one overall party score.
analogies.cars: Imagine combining all stored fuel-efficiency readings equally to produce their average value.
analogies.gardening: Think of mixing equal amounts from every stored soil sample, creating a blend that represents their average.

concepts.title: Dot Product
concepts.summary: The dot product sums products of corresponding elements from two vectors.

provenance.quotedText: Given two vectors , their dot product (also known as inner product, ) is a sum over the products of the elements at the same position: .

lesson.plainTitle: Multiplying Matching Numbers and Adding Them

steps.text: The dot product of two vectors—ordered lists of numbers—is found by multiplying numbers in matching positions and adding those products.

citation.quotedText: The dot product of two vectors is a sum over the products of the elements at the same position

analogies.cooking: Imagine pairing each recipe quantity with its price, multiplying each pair, then adding the costs to get the total.
analogies.sports: Like multiplying each player's score by a matching game count, then adding all those products into one team total.
analogies.music: Imagine pairing each instrument's volume with its playing time, multiplying the matches, then adding the results.
analogies.video-games: Like matching each collected item with its point value, multiplying each pair, then adding everything for a final score.
analogies.cars: Imagine pairing miles driven in each segment with its matching fuel rate, multiplying each pair, then adding the results.
analogies.gardening: Like matching each garden row's plant count with its yield per plant, multiplying each pair, then adding the harvest amounts.

steps.text: A dot product can represent a weighted sum, where each value is multiplied by its assigned weight.

citation.quotedText: the weighted sum of the values in according to the weights could be expressed as the dot product .

analogies.cooking: Imagine rating ingredients by importance: multiply each flavor score by its assigned importance, then add the results for an overall dish score.
analogies.sports: Like giving goals, assists, and saves different importance: multiply each statistic by its weight, then add them for one rating.
analogies.music: Imagine weighting melody, rhythm, and harmony differently, multiplying each score by its importance before adding them into one evaluation.
analogies.video-games: Like multiplying combat, exploration, and puzzle scores by their assigned weights, then adding them for an overall ranking.
analogies.cars: Imagine weighting fuel economy, safety, and comfort differently, multiplying each rating by its importance, then adding them for one score.
analogies.gardening: Like weighting sunlight, soil quality, and watering by importance, multiplying each condition's score by its weight, then adding the results.

steps.text: If the weights are nonnegative and add up to 1, the dot product represents a weighted average.

citation.quotedText: When the weights are nonnegative and sum to 1, i.e., , the dot product expresses a weighted average.

analogies.cooking: Imagine blending juices in proportions totaling one; multiplying each flavor score by its share and adding gives the blend's weighted-average flavor score.
analogies.sports: Like combining performance ratings with nonnegative season shares totaling one; the multiplied-and-added result is a weighted-average rating.
analogies.music: Imagine mixing track volumes using nonnegative shares totaling one; multiplying each track's level by its share and adding gives a weighted average.
analogies.video-games: Like combining level scores with nonnegative importance shares totaling one; multiplying and adding produces a weighted-average score.
analogies.cars: Imagine averaging fuel economy across trips using nonnegative distance shares totaling one; multiply each trip's economy by its share, then add.
analogies.gardening: Like combining plant heights using nonnegative plot shares totaling one; multiplying each height by its share and adding gives a weighted average.

concepts.title: Matrix–Vector Product
concepts.summary: A matrix–vector product produces a vector whose entries are dot products between matrix rows and the input vector.

provenance.quotedText: The matrix--vector product is simply a column vector of length m, whose element is the dot product : We can think of multiplication with a matrix as a transformation that projects vectors from to .

lesson.plainTitle: Multiplying a Matrix by a Vector

steps.text: The result of multiplying a matrix by a vector is a column vector with m entries. Each entry is the dot product of a matrix row and the input vector.

citation.quotedText: The matrix--vector product is simply a column vector of length m, whose element is the dot product

analogies.cooking: Imagine each matrix row as a recipe: combine it with the ingredient vector, and each finished dish becomes one entry in the result.
analogies.sports: Think of each matrix row as a scoring formula applied to player statistics; every formula produces one score in the final column.
analogies.music: Imagine each matrix row as a mixing pattern applied to the same set of tracks; each mix produces one output level.
analogies.video-games: Like applying several damage formulas to one character-stat vector, each matrix row calculates one resulting value.
analogies.cars: Imagine each matrix row as a performance test using the same vehicle measurements; every test yields one entry in the result.
analogies.gardening: Think of each matrix row as a planting plan using the same resource amounts; each plan calculates one expected result.

steps.text: You can think of matrix multiplication as a transformation that projects vectors from one space to another.

citation.quotedText: We can think of multiplication with a matrix as a transformation that projects vectors from to .

analogies.cooking: Imagine a recipe transforming separate ingredients into a plated dish, changing the input collection into a new kind of output.
analogies.sports: Like a coach converting player statistics into role ratings, the transformation maps one set of values into another.
analogies.music: Imagine a sound mixer transforming instrument inputs into speaker outputs, mapping the original levels into a new arrangement.
analogies.video-games: Think of a portal converting a character’s coordinates from one game area into matching coordinates in another.
analogies.cars: Like a transmission converting engine input into wheel output, the transformation maps values from one working space to another.
analogies.gardening: Imagine a garden plan converting available seeds and space into assigned planting areas, mapping inputs into a new layout.

steps.text: For the multiplication to work, the number of columns in the matrix must equal the length of the vector.

citation.quotedText: Note that the column dimension of A (its length along axis 1) must be the same as the dimension of x (its length).

analogies.cooking: Imagine matching every recipe quantity with exactly one ingredient; if the counts differ, the combination cannot be completed.
analogies.sports: Like pairing each player statistic with one scoring weight, the multiplication works only when both lists have matching lengths.
analogies.music: Imagine a mixer with one control for every input track; the setup works only when the number of controls and tracks matches.
analogies.video-games: Like equipping one modifier for each character attribute, the calculation works only when the modifier and attribute counts match.
analogies.cars: Imagine connecting a wiring harness: every matrix column needs a matching vector connection, or the system cannot be joined.
analogies.gardening: Like assigning one watering amount to each garden row, the calculation works only when the numbers of assignments and rows match.

concepts.title: Positional Encoding
concepts.summary: Positional encodings provide token-order information to a model as additional token-associated inputs.

provenance.quotedText: The dominant approach for preserving information about the order of tokens is to represent this to the model as an additional input associated with each token.

lesson.plainTitle: Adding Token Order to a Model

steps.text: The main way to preserve token order is to give the model extra information linked to each token.

citation.quotedText: The dominant approach for preserving information about the order of tokens is to represent this to the model as an additional input associated with each token.

analogies.cooking: Imagine labeling each ingredient with its recipe step, so the cook knows not only what it is but when it belongs.
analogies.sports: Like assigning each player a marked position, extra information shows where each member belongs in the formation.
analogies.music: Imagine adding beat numbers to notes, helping a musician understand their order in the piece.
analogies.video-games: Like placing numbered quest markers beside objectives, extra labels show the sequence in which they appear.
analogies.cars: Imagine tagging each car part with its assembly position, showing where it belongs in the build order.
analogies.gardening: Like marking each seed packet with its row number, extra labels preserve the intended planting arrangement.

steps.text: These extra inputs are called positional encodings, and they may be learned or set in advance.

citation.quotedText: These inputs are called positional encodings, and they can either be learned or fixed a priori.

analogies.cooking: Imagine recipe-step labels that are either developed through practice or printed beforehand; these resemble learned or preset positional encodings.
analogies.sports: Like player-position assignments learned through training or fixed by a coach before the game.
analogies.music: Imagine timing cues that musicians either learn through rehearsal or receive already written in the score.
analogies.video-games: Like map positions discovered by the game system or placed in advance by the level designer.
analogies.cars: Imagine assembly-order tags that are either learned from production patterns or predefined by engineers.
analogies.gardening: Like planting-location markers chosen from experience or arranged beforehand according to a garden plan.

steps.text: One fixed positional encoding method uses sine and cosine functions.

citation.quotedText: We now describe a simple scheme for fixed positional encodings based on sine and cosine functions .

analogies.cooking: Imagine a repeating pair of kitchen timers rising and falling in different rhythms to mark each recipe step distinctly.
analogies.sports: Like two coordinated stadium waves following smooth repeating patterns to signal successive positions around the field.
analogies.music: Imagine two smooth, repeating tones—one following a sine pattern and one a cosine pattern—helping mark each musical position.
analogies.video-games: Like two repeating energy signals that rise and fall differently, giving each location its own combined position marker.
analogies.cars: Imagine two smoothly cycling dashboard needles whose paired readings distinguish each point along a route.
analogies.gardening: Like two repeating sprinkler rhythms, offset from each other, combining to mark different positions across garden rows.

concepts.title: Queries, Keys, and Values
concepts.summary: An attention mechanism uses query–key compatibility to determine weights applied to values.

provenance.quotedText: The attention mechanism computes a linear combination over values via attention pooling, where weights are derived according to the compatibility between a query and keys .

lesson.plainTitle: How Attention Uses Queries, Keys, and Values

steps.text: Attention compares a query with keys to produce weights, then uses those weights to combine the values.

citation.quotedText: The attention mechanism computes a linear combination over values via attention pooling, where weights are derived according to the compatibility between a query and keys .

analogies.cooking: Imagine a chef matching a diner's request to labeled ingredients, scoring each match, then blending ingredient portions according to those scores.
analogies.sports: Think of a coach comparing a game plan with players' strengths, assigning each player a weight, then combining their contributions accordingly.
analogies.music: Imagine matching a desired mood to musical tracks, weighting how well each track fits, then mixing their sounds in those proportions.
analogies.video-games: Like comparing a mission objective with each character's abilities, assigning match weights, then combining the characters' useful actions accordingly.
analogies.cars: Imagine matching a driving need to vehicle features, weighting each feature's fit, then combining their contributions into a final choice.
analogies.gardening: Think of matching a garden's needs to plant labels, assigning fit weights, then combining selected plants according to those weights.

steps.text: When the weights are nonnegative and add up to 1, larger weights can be viewed as selecting more relevant parts—but this is only an intuition.

citation.quotedText: In this case we might interpret large weights as a way for the model to select components of relevance. While this is a good intuition, it is important to remember that it is just that, an intuition.

analogies.cooking: Imagine recipe portions that are never negative and fill one whole bowl; larger portions suggest more relevant ingredients, though that is only a helpful picture.
analogies.sports: Think of playing-time shares totaling the whole match; larger shares suggest more relevant players, though the comparison is only an intuition.
analogies.music: Imagine mixer levels expressed as nonnegative shares of one whole mix; louder shares suggest more relevant parts, but this is only a mental model.
analogies.video-games: Think of a fixed pool of skill points split without negatives; more points suggest a more relevant ability, though that interpretation is only intuitive.
analogies.cars: Imagine dividing total traction among four wheels with nonnegative shares; a larger share suggests greater relevance, but that is only a simplifying picture.
analogies.gardening: Think of dividing one watering can into nonnegative portions; plants receiving larger portions seem more relevant, though that is only an intuition.

concepts.title: Self-Attention
concepts.summary: Self-attention lets every token attend to every other token when constructing token representations.

provenance.quotedText: Because every token is attending to each other token (unlike the case where decoder steps attend to encoder steps), such architectures are typically described as self-attention models , and elsewhere described as intra-attention model .

lesson.plainTitle: How Each Token Looks at the Others

steps.text: In self-attention, every token pays attention to every other token.

citation.quotedText: Because every token is attending to each other token (unlike the case where decoder steps attend to encoder steps), such architectures are typically described as self-attention models , and elsewhere described as intra-attention model .

analogies.cooking: Imagine every ingredient considering every other ingredient before contributing its flavor to the dish.
analogies.sports: Like every player watching all teammates and opponents before deciding the next move.
analogies.music: Imagine every note listening to all the other notes to understand its role in the phrase.
analogies.video-games: Think of every character checking all other characters before choosing an action.
analogies.cars: Imagine every car in traffic noticing all the other cars before adjusting its movement.
analogies.gardening: Like every plant being considered alongside all the others when planning the garden.

steps.text: Self-attention produces an output sequence with the same length as the input sequence.

citation.quotedText: Given a sequence of input tokens where any ( ), its self-attention outputs a sequence of the same length

analogies.cooking: Like a tray where each original ingredient gets one prepared portion, leaving the number of portions unchanged.
analogies.sports: Imagine every player receiving an updated assignment, while the team still has the same number of players.
analogies.music: Like each input note becoming a refined note, with the melody keeping the same number of notes.
analogies.video-games: Imagine each character receiving an updated status, without adding or removing characters.
analogies.cars: Like every car receiving updated navigation guidance, while the number of cars remains unchanged.
analogies.gardening: Imagine each plant getting an updated care plan, while the garden keeps the same number of plants.

steps.text: Self-attention can also use added information about the order of the sequence.

citation.quotedText: In this section, we will discuss sequence encoding using self-attention, including using additional information for the sequence order.

analogies.cooking: Imagine adding recipe-step numbers so ingredients are understood by when they enter the dish.
analogies.sports: Like using player positions and play order to clarify how everyone fits into the strategy.
analogies.music: Imagine marking each note's beat so the sequence's order helps shape the performance.
analogies.video-games: Like adding map positions or turn numbers so actions are understood in sequence.
analogies.cars: Imagine route markers showing where each car is along a journey, adding order to traffic information.
analogies.gardening: Like labeling planting rows and dates so each plant's place in the garden's sequence is clear.

concepts.title: Softmax
concepts.summary: Softmax transforms neural network outputs into a valid discrete probability distribution.

provenance.quotedText: As a side effect, we encountered the softmax, a convenient activation function that transforms outputs of an ordinary neural network layer into valid discrete probability distributions.

steps.text: The main idea is this: Softmax transforms neural network outputs into a valid discrete probability distribution.

citation.quotedText: As a side effect, we encountered the softmax, a convenient activation function that transforms outputs of an ordinary neural network layer into valid discrete probability distributions.

analogies.cooking: Imagine dividing a fixed pie among several dishes: stronger scores receive larger slices, while all slices together make one whole pie.
analogies.sports: Like converting team ratings into win chances: higher-rated teams get larger percentages, and all percentages add up to 100%.
analogies.music: Imagine turning each instrument’s volume score into a share of the mix, with every share combining into one complete track.
analogies.video-games: Like converting character scores into selection chances: stronger scores mean higher odds, while all character probabilities total 100%.
analogies.cars: Imagine turning several cars’ performance scores into purchase probabilities, with higher scores receiving larger shares that together total 100%.
analogies.gardening: Like distributing one full watering allowance among plants according to their need scores, so every plant gets a share and the shares total 100%.

steps.text: Keep this idea in mind: Softmax transforms neural network outputs into a valid discrete probability distribution.

citation.quotedText: As a side effect, we encountered the softmax, a convenient activation function that transforms outputs of an ordinary neural network layer into valid discrete probability distributions.

analogies.cooking: Think of ranking recipe scores, then converting them into portions of one tasting menu: favored recipes get larger portions, but the total remains one menu.
analogies.sports: Imagine translating player scores into draft probabilities: top players receive higher chances, while every player’s chance fits within one 100% total.
analogies.music: Like turning song preference scores into play probabilities, where higher-scoring songs are more likely and all song chances add to 100%.
analogies.video-games: Imagine transforming loot scores into drop chances: better-scoring items get greater odds, while all possible drops form one complete probability distribution.
analogies.cars: Like converting route scores into navigation choices: the best-looking route gets the highest probability, and all route probabilities sum to 100%.
analogies.gardening: Imagine changing seed scores into planting probabilities: promising seeds receive higher chances, while all choices together account for the entire 100%.

concepts.title: Softmax Preserves Ordering
concepts.summary: Softmax preserves the ordering of its inputs, so the largest input also identifies the highest-probability class.

provenance.quotedText: Moreover, because the softmax operation preserves the ordering among its arguments, we do not need to compute the softmax to determine which class has been assigned the highest probability.

lesson.plainTitle: Softmax Keeps Scores in the Same Order

steps.text: Softmax keeps its input values in the same order.

citation.quotedText: the softmax operation preserves the ordering among its arguments

analogies.cooking: Imagine ranking dishes by their original tasting scores; softmax changes the scores’ format, but the first-, second-, and third-place dishes stay ordered.
analogies.sports: Like converting race results into percentages while keeping every athlete in the same finishing order.
analogies.music: Imagine turning song ratings into shares of audience preference; the highest-rated song remains first, and the others keep their ranking.
analogies.video-games: Like converting player scores into probability bars without changing who ranks above whom on the leaderboard.
analogies.cars: Imagine expressing car performance scores as proportions; the fastest-rated car stays on top, with the remaining order unchanged.
analogies.gardening: Like turning flower-growth measurements into percentage shares while keeping the tallest-to-shortest ranking the same.

steps.text: You can identify the class with the highest probability without calculating softmax.

citation.quotedText: we do not need to compute the softmax to determine which class has been assigned the highest probability

analogies.cooking: Imagine choosing the winning dish by spotting the highest tasting score; you need not convert every score into percentages first.
analogies.sports: Like identifying the race leader from the highest point total without calculating each athlete’s share of all points.
analogies.music: Imagine selecting the top song directly from the highest rating instead of first converting every rating into preference percentages.
analogies.video-games: Like finding the winning player by checking the largest score, without calculating probability bars for the whole leaderboard.
analogies.cars: Imagine picking the top-ranked car from the largest performance score without converting all car scores into proportions.
analogies.gardening: Like identifying the tallest plant from its measurement without calculating each plant’s percentage of the group’s total height.

concepts.title: Vector Norm
concepts.summary: A vector norm measures the magnitude or size of a vector.

provenance.quotedText: Informally, the norm of a vector tells us how big it is.

lesson.plainTitle: How a Vector’s Size Is Measured

steps.text: A vector norm tells us how big a vector is.

citation.quotedText: Informally, the norm of a vector tells us how big it is.

analogies.cooking: Imagine a recipe’s overall intensity summarized by one score; a vector norm similarly gives one measure of how big the vector is.
analogies.sports: Think of combining a player’s performance numbers into one overall rating; a vector norm similarly summarizes a vector’s size.
analogies.music: Like reducing several volume levels to one overall loudness score, a vector norm expresses a vector’s size with one value.
analogies.video-games: Imagine a character’s movement across several directions summarized as one movement size; a vector norm provides that single measure.
analogies.cars: Think of several motion components summarized by one measure of overall movement; a vector norm similarly reports a vector’s size.
analogies.gardening: Like combining several plant-growth measurements into one overall growth score, a vector norm summarizes how big a vector is.

steps.text: This size concerns the magnitude of the vector’s components, not how many dimensions it has.

citation.quotedText: Here, we are employing a notion of size that concerns the magnitude of a vector's components (not its dimensionality).

analogies.cooking: Imagine two dishes with the same number of ingredients but different quantities; size depends on the amounts, not the ingredient count.
analogies.sports: Like two teams with equal roster sizes but different player statistics, overall magnitude comes from the values, not how many categories exist.
analogies.music: Think of two chords with the same number of notes but different volumes; their size reflects note strengths, not the number of notes.
analogies.video-games: Imagine two inventories with the same number of slots but different item stacks; total size depends on amounts, not slot count.
analogies.cars: Like two engines with the same number of measured features but different readings, magnitude depends on the readings, not the feature count.
analogies.gardening: Imagine two beds with the same number of plant types but different growth amounts; size concerns those amounts, not the number of types.

steps.text: Different valid norms can represent different ideas of size.

citation.quotedText: Many functions are valid norms and different norms encode different notions of size.

analogies.cooking: Imagine judging a meal by total calories or by its strongest flavor; different valid measures highlight different ideas of overall size.
analogies.sports: Like rating an athlete by total performance or their strongest event, different scoring methods can capture different meanings of size.
analogies.music: Think of measuring a song by combined volume or its loudest note; different valid measures emphasize different ideas of size.
analogies.video-games: Imagine evaluating gear by total bonuses or the largest single bonus; different valid methods represent size in different ways.
analogies.cars: Like assessing a car by combined performance figures or its strongest specification, different valid measures can express different notions of size.
analogies.gardening: Imagine judging a garden by total growth or its tallest plant; different valid measures can represent different ideas of size.

concepts.title: Vectors
concepts.summary: A vector is a fixed-length array whose elements are scalar values.

provenance.quotedText: For current purposes, you can think of a vector as a fixed-length array of scalars.

steps.text: A vector is a fixed-length list of scalars, which are single values such as numbers.

citation.quotedText: For current purposes, you can think of a vector as a fixed-length array of scalars.

analogies.cooking: Imagine a recipe tray with a fixed number of slots, each holding one measured amount such as 2 cups or 5 grams.
analogies.sports: Think of a player stat line with a fixed number of spaces, each containing one number such as points, assists, or rebounds.
analogies.music: Imagine a short melody pattern with a fixed number of positions, each holding one numeric value for pitch or duration.
analogies.video-games: Like a character status bar with a fixed number of slots, each storing one number such as health, armor, or speed.
analogies.cars: Imagine a dashboard record with a fixed number of spaces, each containing one value such as speed, fuel level, or mileage.
analogies.gardening: Think of a planting row with a fixed number of pots, each labeled by one number such as water amount or plant height.

steps.text: The scalar values in a vector are called its elements, entries, or components.

citation.quotedText: As with their code counterparts, we call these scalars the elements of the vector (synonyms include entries and components).

analogies.cooking: Imagine each measured amount in a recipe tray being called an item, entry, or component of the complete set.
analogies.sports: Think of points, assists, and rebounds as individual entries or components within one player's stat line.
analogies.music: Imagine every numeric pitch or duration as an element, entry, or component of a melody pattern.
analogies.video-games: Like health, armor, and speed being separate elements or components in a character's fixed status list.
analogies.cars: Imagine speed, fuel level, and mileage as individual entries or components in one dashboard record.
analogies.gardening: Think of each pot's water or height number as an element, entry, or component of the garden record.

steps.text: When a vector represents a real-world example, each component can correspond to a quantity such as income, employment length, or previous loan defaults.

citation.quotedText: For example, if we were training a model to predict the risk of a loan defaulting, we might associate each applicant with a vector whose components correspond to quantities like their income, length of employment, or number of previous defaults.

analogies.cooking: Imagine a recipe vector whose components track flour amount, baking time, and oven temperature, with each position representing a different quantity.
analogies.sports: Think of an athlete vector where components represent points scored, minutes played, and fouls committed.
analogies.music: Imagine a song vector whose components correspond to tempo, duration, and volume, each describing one quantity.
analogies.video-games: Like a character vector whose components represent health, experience points, and completed quests.
analogies.cars: Imagine a vehicle vector where components correspond to speed, fuel remaining, and distance traveled.
analogies.gardening: Think of a plant vector whose components represent height, days since watering, and number of blossoms.

sources.title: Dive into Deep Learning — 2.3 Linear Algebra
sources.author: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, and Alexander J. Smola
sources.text: # Linear Algebra By now, we can load datasets into tensors and manipulate these tensors with basic mathematical operations. To start building sophisticated models, we will also need a few tools from linear algebra. This section offers a gentle introduction to the most essential concepts, starting from scalar arithmetic and ramping up to matrix multiplication. ## Scalars Most everyday mathematics consists of manipulating numbers one at a time. Formally, we call these values scalars. For example, the temperature in Palo Alto is a balmy 72 degrees Fahrenheit. If you wanted to convert the temperature to Celsius you would evaluate the expression , setting f to 72. In this equation, the values 5, 9, and 32 are constant scalars. The variables c and f in general represent unknown scalars. We denote scalars by ordinary lower-cased letters (e.g., x, y, and z) and the space of all (continuous) real-valued scalars by . For expedience, we will skip past rigorous definitions of spaces: just remember that the expression is a formal way to say that x is a real-valued scalar. The symbol (pronounced "in") denotes membership in a set. For example, indicates that x and y are variables that can only take values 0 or 1. (Scalars are implemented as tensors that contain only one element.) Below, we assign two scalars and perform the familiar addition, multiplication, division, and exponentiation operations. ## Vectors For current purposes, you can think of a vector as a fixed-length array of scalars. As with their code counterparts, we call these scalars the elements of the vector (synonyms include entries and components). When vectors represent examples from real-world datasets, their values hold some real-world significance. For example, if we were training a model to predict the risk of a loan defaulting, we might associate each applicant with a vector whose components correspond to quantities like their income, length of employment, or number of previous defaults. If we were studying the risk of heart attack, each vector might represent a patient and its components might correspond to their most recent vital signs, cholesterol levels, minutes of exercise per day, etc. We denote vectors by bold lowercase letters, (e.g., , , and ). Vectors are implemented as -order tensors. In general, such tensors can have arbitrary lengths, subject to memory limitations. Caution: in Python, as in most programming languages, vector indices start at 0, also known as zero-based indexing, whereas in linear algebra subscripts begin at 1 (one-based indexing). We can refer to an element of a vector by using a subscript. For example, denotes the second element of . Since is a scalar, we do not bold it. By default, we visualize vectors by stacking their elements vertically. Here are elements of the vector. Later on, we will distinguish between such column vectors and row vectors whose elements are stacked horizontally. Recall that we access a tensor's elements via indexing. To indicate that a vector contains n elements, we write . Formally, we call n the dimensionality of the vector. In code, this corresponds to the tensor's length, accessible via Python's built-in len function. We can also access the length via the shape attribute. The shape is a tuple that indicates a tensor's length along each axis. (Tensors with just one axis have shapes with just one element.) Oftentimes, the word "dimension" gets overloaded to mean both the number of axes and the length along a particular axis. To avoid this confusion, we use order to refer to the number of axes and dimensionality exclusively to refer to the number of components. ## Matrices Just as scalars are -order tensors and vectors are -order tensors, matrices are -order tensors. We denote matrices by bold capital letters (e.g., , , and ), and represent them in code by tensors with two axes. The expression indicates that a matrix contains real-valued scalars, arranged as m rows and n columns. When , we say that a matrix is square. Visually, we can illustrate any matrix as a table. To refer to an individual element, we subscript both the row and column indices, e.g., is the value that belongs to 's row and column: In code, we represent a matrix by a -order tensor with shape (m, n). We can convert any appropriately sized tensor into an matrix by passing the desired shape to reshape: Sometimes we want to flip the axes. When we exchange a matrix's rows and columns, the result is called its transpose. Formally, we signify a matrix 's transpose by and if , then for all i and j. Thus, the transpose of an matrix is an matrix: In code, we can access any (matrix's transpose) as follows: Symmetric matrices are the subset of square matrices that are equal to their own transposes: . The following matrix is symmetric: Matrices are useful for representing datasets. Typically, rows correspond to individual records and columns correspond to distinct attributes. ## Tensors While you can go far in your machine learning journey with only scalars, vectors, and matrices, eventually you may need to work with higher-order tensors. Tensors (give us a generic way of describing extensions to -order arrays.) We call software objects of the tensor class "tensors" precisely because they too can have arbitrary numbers of axes. While it may be confusing to use the word tensor for both the mathematical object and its realization in code, our meaning should usually be clear from context. We denote general tensors by capital letters with a special font face (e.g., , , and ) and their indexing mechanism (e.g., and ) follows naturally from that of matrices. Tensors will become more important when we start working with images. Each image arrives as a -order tensor with axes corresponding to the height, width, and channel. At each spatial location, the intensities of each color (red, green, and blue) are stacked along the channel. Furthermore, a collection of images is represented in code by a -order tensor, where distinct images are indexed along the first axis. Higher-order tensors are constructed, as were vectors and matrices, by growing the number of shape components. ## Basic Properties of Tensor Arithmetic Scalars, vectors, matrices, and higher-order tensors all have some handy properties. For example, elementwise operations produce outputs that have the same shape as their operands. The elementwise product of two matrices is called their Hadamard product (denoted ). We can spell out the entries of the Hadamard product of two matrices : Adding or multiplying a scalar and a tensor produces a result with the same shape as the original tensor. Here, each element of the tensor is added to (or multiplied by) the scalar. ## Reduction Often, we wish to calculate the sum of a tensor's elements. To express the sum of the elements in a vector of length n, we write . There is a simple function for it: To express sums over the elements of tensors of arbitrary shape, we simply sum over all its axes. For example, the sum of the elements of an matrix could be written . By default, invoking the sum function reduces a tensor along all of its axes, eventually producing a scalar. Our libraries also allow us to specify the axes along which the tensor should be reduced. To sum over all elements along the rows (axis 0), we specify axis=0 in sum. Since the input matrix reduces along axis 0 to generate the output vector, this axis is missing from the shape of the output. Specifying axis=1 will reduce the column dimension (axis 1) by summing up elements of all the columns. Reducing a matrix along both rows and columns via summation is equivalent to summing up all the elements of the matrix. A related quantity is the mean, also called the average. We calculate the mean by dividing the sum by the total number of elements. Because computing the mean is so common, it gets a dedicated library function that works analogously to sum. Likewise, the function for calculating the mean can also reduce a tensor along specific axes. ## Non-Reduction Sum Sometimes it can be useful to keep the number of axes unchanged when invoking the function for calculating the sum or mean. This matters when we want to use the broadcast mechanism. For instance, since sum_A keeps its two axes after summing each row, we can (divide A by sum_A with broadcasting) to create a matrix where each row sums up to 1. If we want to calculate the cumulative sum of elements of A along some axis, say axis=0 (row by row), we can call the cumsum function. By design, this function does not reduce the input tensor along any axis. ## Dot Products So far, we have only performed elementwise operations, sums, and averages. And if this was all we could do, linear algebra would not deserve its own section. Fortunately, this is where things get more interesting. One of the most fundamental operations is the dot product. Given two vectors , their dot product (also known as inner product, ) is a sum over the products of the elements at the same position: . The dot product of two vectors is a sum over the products of the elements at the same position Equivalently, (we can calculate the dot product of two vectors by performing an elementwise multiplication followed by a sum:) Dot products are useful in a wide range of contexts. For example, given some set of values, denoted by a vector , and a set of weights, denoted by , the weighted sum of the values in according to the weights could be expressed as the dot product . When the weights are nonnegative and sum to 1, i.e., , the dot product expresses a weighted average. After normalizing two vectors to have unit length, the dot products express the cosine of the angle between them. Later in this section, we will formally introduce this notion of length. ## Matrix--Vector Products Now that we know how to calculate dot products, we can begin to understand the product between an matrix and an n-dimensional vector . To start off, we visualize our matrix in terms of its row vectors where each is a row vector representing the row of the matrix . The matrix--vector product is simply a column vector of length m, whose element is the dot product : We can think of multiplication with a matrix as a transformation that projects vectors from to . These transformations are remarkably useful. For example, we can represent rotations as multiplications by certain square matrices. Matrix--vector products also describe the key calculation involved in computing the outputs of each layer in a neural network given the outputs from the previous layer. :begin_tab:mxnet To express a matrix--vector product in code, we use the same dot function. The operation is inferred based on the type of the arguments. Note that the column dimension of A (its length along axis 1) must be the same as the dimension of x (its length). :end_tab: :begin_tab:pytorch To express a matrix--vector product in code, we use the mv function. Note that the column dimension of A (its length along axis 1) must be the same as the dimension of x (its length). Python has a convenience operator @ that can execute both matrix--vector and matrix--matrix products (depending on its arguments). Thus we can write A@x. :end_tab: :begin_tab:tensorflow To express a matrix--vector product in code, we use the matvec function. Note that the column dimension of A (its length along axis 1) must be the same as the dimension of x (its length). :end_tab: ## Matrix--Matrix Multiplication Once you have gotten the hang of dot products and matrix--vector products, then matrix--matrix multiplication should be straightforward. Say that we have two matrices and : Let denote the row vector representing the row of the matrix and let denote the column vector from the column of the matrix : To form the matrix product , we simply compute each element as the dot product between the row of and the column of , i.e., : We can think of the matrix--matrix multiplication as performing m matrix--vector products or dot products and stitching the results together to form an matrix. In the following snippet, we perform matrix multiplication on A and B. Here, A is a matrix with two rows and three columns, and B is a matrix with three rows and four columns. After multiplication, we obtain a matrix with two rows and four columns. The term matrix--matrix multiplication is often simplified to matrix multiplication, and should not be confused with the Hadamard product. ## Norms Some of the most useful operators in linear algebra are norms. Informally, the norm of a vector tells us how big it is. For instance, the norm measures the (Euclidean) length of a vector. Here, we are employing a notion of size that concerns the magnitude of a vector's components (not its dimensionality). A norm is a function that maps a vector to a scalar and satisfies the following three properties: 1. Given any vector , if we scale (all elements of) the vector by a scalar , its norm scales accordingly: 2. For any vectors and : norms satisfy the triangle inequality: 3. The norm of a vector is nonnegative and it only vanishes if the vector is zero: Many functions are valid norms and different norms encode different notions of size. The Euclidean norm that we all learned in elementary school geometry when calculating the hypotenuse of a right triangle is the square root of the sum of squares of a vector's elements. Formally, this is called the norm and expressed as ( ) The method norm calculates the norm. The norm is also common and the associated measure is called the Manhattan distance. By definition, the norm sums the absolute values of a vector's elements: ( ) Compared to the norm, it is less sensitive to outliers. To compute the norm, we compose the absolute value with the sum operation. Both the and norms are special cases of the more general norms: In the case of matrices, matters are more complicated. After all, matrices can be viewed both as collections of individual entries and as objects that operate on vectors and transform them into other vectors. For instance, we can ask by how much longer the matrix--vector product could be relative to . This line of thought leads to what is called the spectral norm. For now, we introduce the Frobenius norm, which is much easier to compute and defined as the square root of the sum of the squares of a matrix's elements: The Frobenius norm behaves as if it were an norm of a matrix-shaped vector. Invoking the following function will calculate the Frobenius norm of a matrix. While we do not want to get too far ahead of ourselves, we already can plant some intuition about why these concepts are useful. In deep learning, we are often trying to solve optimization problems: maximize the probability assigned to observed data; maximize the revenue associated with a recommender model; minimize the distance between predictions and the ground truth observations; minimize the distance between representations of photos of the same person while maximizing the distance between representations of photos of different people. These distances, which constitute the objectives of deep learning algorithms, are often expressed as norms. ## Discussion In this section, we have reviewed all the linear algebra that you will need to understand a significant chunk of modern deep learning. There is a lot more to linear algebra, though, and much of it is useful for machine learning. For example, matrices can be decomposed into factors, and these decompositions can reveal low-dimensional structure in real-world datasets. There are entire subfields of machine learning that focus on using matrix decompositions and their generalizations to high-order tensors to discover structure in datasets and solve prediction problems. But this book focuses on deep learning. And we believe you will be more inclined to learn more mathematics once you have gotten your hands dirty applying machine learning to real datasets. So while we reserve the right to introduce more mathematics later on, we wrap up this section here. If you are eager to learn more linear algebra, there are many excellent books and online resources. For a more advanced crash course, consider checking out , , and . To recap: Scalars, vectors, matrices, and tensors are the basic mathematical objects used in linear algebra and have zero, one, two, and an arbitrary number of axes, respectively. Tensors can be sliced or reduced along specified axes via indexing, or operations such as sum and mean, respectively. Elementwise products are called Hadamard products. By contrast, dot products, matrix--vector products, and matrix--matrix products are not elementwise operations and in general return objects having shapes that are different from the the operands. Compared to Hadamard products, matrix--matrix products take considerably longer to compute (cubic rather than quadratic time). Norms capture various notions of the magnitude of a vector (or matrix), and are commonly applied to the difference of two vectors to measure their distance apart. Common vector norms include the and norms, and common matrix norms include the spectral and Frobenius norms. ## Exercises 1. Prove that the transpose of the transpose of a matrix is the matrix itself: . 1. Given two matrices and , show that sum and transposition commute: . 1. Given any square matrix , is always symmetric? Can you prove the result by using only the results of the previous two exercises? 1. We defined the tensor X of shape (2, 3, 4) in this section. What is the output of len(X)? Write your answer without implementing any code, then check your answer using code. 1. For a tensor X of arbitrary shape, does len(X) always correspond to the length of a certain axis of X? What is that axis? 1. Run A / A.sum(axis=1) and see what happens. Can you analyze the results? 1. When traveling between two points in downtown Manhattan, what is the distance that you need to cover in terms of the coordinates, i.e., in terms of avenues and streets? Can you travel diagonally? 1. Consider a tensor of shape (2, 3, 4). What are the shapes of the summation outputs along axes 0, 1, and 2? 1. Feed a tensor with three or more axes to the linalg.norm function and observe its output. What does this function compute for tensors of arbitrary shape? 1. Consider three large matrices, say , and , initialized with Gaussian random variables. You want to compute the product . Is there any difference in memory footprint and speed, depending on whether you compute or . Why? 1. Consider three large matrices, say , and . Is there any difference in speed depending on whether you compute or ? Why? What changes if you initialize without cloning memory? Why? 1. Consider three matrices, say . Construct a tensor with three axes by stacking . What is the dimensionality? Slice out the second coordinate of the third axis to recover . Check that your answer is correct. :begin_tab:mxnet Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:pytorch Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:tensorflow Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:jax Discussions :end_tab:

sources.title: Dive into Deep Learning — 11.1 Queries, Keys, and Values
sources.author: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, and Alexander J. Smola
sources.text: # Queries, Keys, and Values So far all the networks we have reviewed crucially relied on the input being of a well-defined size. For instance, the images in ImageNet are of size pixels and CNNs are specifically tuned to this size. Even in natural language processing the input size for RNNs is well defined and fixed. Variable size is addressed by sequentially processing one token at a time, or by specially designed convolution kernels . This approach can lead to significant problems when the input is truly of varying size with varying information content, such as in in the transformation of text . In particular, for long sequences it becomes quite difficult to keep track of everything that has already been generated or even viewed by the network. Even explicit tracking heuristics such as proposed by only offer limited benefit. Compare this to databases. In their simplest form they are collections of keys (k) and values (v). For instance, our database might consist of tuples \{("Zhang", "Aston"), ("Lipton", "Zachary"), ("Li", "Mu"), ("Smola", "Alex"), ("Hu", "Rachel"), ("Werness", "Brent")\} with the last name being the key and the first name being the value. We can operate on , for instance with the exact query (q) for "Li" which would return the value "Mu". If ("Li", "Mu") was not a record in , there would be no valid answer. If we also allowed for approximate matches, we would retrieve ("Lipton", "Zachary") instead. This quite simple and trivial example nonetheless teaches us a number of useful things: We can design queries q that operate on (k,v) pairs in such a manner as to be valid regardless of the database size. The same query can receive different answers, according to the contents of the database. The "code" being executed for operating on a large state space (the database) can be quite simple (e.g., exact match, approximate match, top-k). There is no need to compress or simplify the database to make the operations effective. Clearly we would not have introduced a simple database here if it wasn't for the purpose of explaining deep learning. Indeed, this leads to one of the most exciting concepts introduced in deep learning in the past decade: the attention mechanism . We will cover the specifics of its application to machine translation later. For now, simply consider the following: denote by a database of m tuples of keys and values. Moreover, denote by a query. Then we can define the attention over as where ( ) are scalar attention weights. The operation itself is typically referred to as attention pooling. The name attention derives from the fact that the operation pays particular attention to the terms for which the weight is significant (i.e., large). As such, the attention over generates a linear combination of values contained in the database. In fact, this contains the above example as a special case where all but one weight is zero. We have a number of special cases: The weights are nonnegative. In this case the output of the attention mechanism is contained in the convex cone spanned by the values . The weights form a convex combination, i.e., and for all i. This is the most common setting in deep learning. Exactly one of the weights is 1, while all others are 0. This is akin to a traditional database query. All weights are equal, i.e., for all i. This amounts to averaging across the entire database, also called average pooling in deep learning. A common strategy for ensuring that the weights sum up to 1 is to normalize them via In particular, to ensure that the weights are also nonnegative, one can resort to exponentiation. This means that we can now pick any function and then apply the softmax operation used for multinomial models to it via This operation is readily available in all deep learning frameworks. It is differentiable and its gradient never vanishes, all of which are desirable properties in a model. Note though, the attention mechanism introduced above is not the only option. For instance, we can design a non-differentiable attention model that can be trained using reinforcement learning methods . As one would expect, training such a model is quite complex. Consequently the bulk of modern attention research follows the framework outlined in . We thus focus our exposition on this family of differentiable mechanisms. The attention mechanism computes a linear combination over values via attention pooling, where weights are derived according to the compatibility between a query and keys . What is quite remarkable is that the actual "code" for executing on the set of keys and values, namely the query, can be quite concise, even though the space to operate on is significant. This is a desirable property for a network layer as it does not require too many parameters to learn. Just as convenient is the fact that attention can operate on arbitrarily large databases without the need to change the way the attention pooling operation is performed. ## Visualization One of the benefits of the attention mechanism is that it can be quite intuitive, particularly when the weights are nonnegative and sum to 1. In this case we might interpret large weights as a way for the model to select components of relevance. While this is a good intuition, it is important to remember that it is just that, an intuition. Regardless, we may want to visualize its effect on the given set of keys when applying a variety of different queries. This function will come in handy later. We thus define the show_heatmaps function. Note that it does not take a matrix (of attention weights) as its input but rather a tensor with four axes, allowing for an array of different queries and weights. Consequently the input matrices has the shape (number of rows for display, number of columns for display, number of queries, number of keys). This will come in handy later on when we want to visualize the workings that are to design Transformers. As a quick sanity check let's visualize the identity matrix, representing a case where the attention weight is 1 only when the query and the key are the same. ## Summary The attention mechanism allows us to aggregate data from many (key, value) pairs. So far our discussion was quite abstract, simply describing a way to pool data. We have not explained yet where those mysterious queries, keys, and values might arise from. Some intuition might help here: for instance, in a regression setting, the query might correspond to the location where the regression should be carried out. The keys are the locations where past data was observed and the values are the (regression) values themselves. This is the so-called Nadaraya--Watson estimator that we will be studying in the next section. By design, the attention mechanism provides a differentiable means of control by which a neural network can select elements from a set and to construct an associated weighted sum over representations. ## Exercises 1. Suppose that you wanted to reimplement approximate (key, query) matches as used in classical databases, which attention function would you pick? 1. Suppose that the attention function is given by and that for . Denote by the probability distribution over keys when using the softmax normalization in . Prove that . 1. Design a differentiable search engine using the attention mechanism. 1. Review the design of the Squeeze and Excitation Networks and interpret them through the lens of the attention mechanism. :begin_tab:mxnet Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:pytorch Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:tensorflow Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:jax Discussions :end_tab:

sources.title: Dive into Deep Learning — 11.6 Self-Attention and Positional Encoding
sources.author: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, and Alexander J. Smola
sources.text: # Self-Attention and Positional Encoding In deep learning, we often use CNNs or RNNs to encode sequences. Now with attention mechanisms in mind, imagine feeding a sequence of tokens into an attention mechanism such that at every step, each token has its own query, keys, and values. Here, when computing the value of a token's representation at the next layer, the token can attend (via its query vector) to any other's token (matching based on their key vectors). Using the full set of query-key compatibility scores, we can compute, for each token, a representation by building the appropriate weighted sum over the other tokens. Because every token is attending to each other token (unlike the case where decoder steps attend to encoder steps), such architectures are typically described as self-attention models , and elsewhere described as intra-attention model . In this section, we will discuss sequence encoding using self-attention, including using additional information for the sequence order. ## Self-Attention Given a sequence of input tokens where any ( ), its self-attention outputs a sequence of the same length , where according to the definition of attention pooling in . Using multi-head attention, the following code snippet computes the self-attention of a tensor with shape (batch size, number of time steps or sequence length in tokens, d). The output tensor has the same shape. ## Comparing CNNs, RNNs, and Self-Attention Let's compare architectures for mapping a sequence of n tokens to another one of equal length, where each input or output token is represented by a d-dimensional vector. Specifically, we will consider CNNs, RNNs, and self-attention. We will compare their computational complexity, sequential operations, and maximum path lengths. Note that sequential operations prevent parallel computation, while a shorter path between any combination of sequence positions makes it easier to learn long-range dependencies within the sequence . Comparing CNN (padding tokens are omitted), RNN, and self-attention architectures. Let's regard any text sequence as a "one-dimensional image". Similarly, one-dimensional CNNs can process local features such as n-grams in text. Given a sequence of length n, consider a convolutional layer whose kernel size is k, and whose numbers of input and output channels are both d. The computational complexity of the convolutional layer is . As shows, CNNs are hierarchical, so there are sequential operations and the maximum path length is . For example, and are within the receptive field of a two-layer CNN with kernel size 3 in . When updating the hidden state of RNNs, multiplication of the weight matrix and the d-dimensional hidden state has a computational complexity of . Since the sequence length is n, the computational complexity of the recurrent layer is . According to , there are sequential operations that cannot be parallelized and the maximum path length is also . In self-attention, the queries, keys, and values are all matrices. Consider the scaled dot product attention in , where an matrix is multiplied by a matrix, then the output matrix is multiplied by an matrix. As a result, the self-attention has a computational complexity. As we can see from , each token is directly connected to any other token via self-attention. Therefore, computation can be parallel with sequential operations and the maximum path length is also . All in all, both CNNs and self-attention enjoy parallel computation and self-attention has the shortest maximum path length. However, the quadratic computational complexity with respect to the sequence length makes self-attention prohibitively slow for very long sequences. ## Positional Encoding Unlike RNNs, which recurrently process tokens of a sequence one-by-one, self-attention ditches sequential operations in favor of parallel computation. Note that self-attention by itself does not preserve the order of the sequence. What do we do if it really matters that the model knows in which order the input sequence arrived? The dominant approach for preserving information about the order of tokens is to represent this to the model as an additional input associated with each token. These inputs are called positional encodings, and they can either be learned or fixed a priori. We now describe a simple scheme for fixed positional encodings based on sine and cosine functions . Suppose that the input representation contains the d-dimensional embeddings for n tokens of a sequence. The positional encoding outputs using a positional embedding matrix of the same shape, whose element on the row and the or the column is At first glance, this trigonometric function design looks weird. Before we give explanations of this design, let's first implement it in the following PositionalEncoding class. In the positional embedding matrix , rows correspond to positions within a sequence and columns represent different positional encoding dimensions. In the example below, we can see that the and the columns of the positional embedding matrix have a higher frequency than the and the columns. The offset between the and the (same for the and the ) columns is due to the alternation of sine and cosine functions. ### Absolute Positional Information To see how the monotonically decreased frequency along the encoding dimension relates to absolute positional information, let's print out the binary representations of . As we can see, the lowest bit, the second-lowest bit, and the third-lowest bit alternate on every number, every two numbers, and every four numbers, respectively. In binary representations, a higher bit has a lower frequency than a lower bit. Similarly, as demonstrated in the heat map below, the positional encoding decreases frequencies along the encoding dimension by using trigonometric functions. Since the outputs are float numbers, such continuous representations are more space-efficient than binary representations. ### Relative Positional Information Besides capturing absolute positional information, the above positional encoding also allows a model to easily learn to attend by relative positions. This is because for any fixed position offset , the positional encoding at position can be represented by a linear projection of that at position i. This projection can be explained mathematically. Denoting , any pair of in can be linearly projected to for any fixed offset : where the projection matrix does not depend on any position index i. ## Summary In self-attention, the queries, keys, and values all come from the same place. Both CNNs and self-attention enjoy parallel computation and self-attention has the shortest maximum path length. However, the quadratic computational complexity with respect to the sequence length makes self-attention prohibitively slow for very long sequences. To use the sequence order information, we can inject absolute or relative positional information by adding positional encoding to the input representations. ## Exercises 1. Suppose that we design a deep architecture to represent a sequence by stacking self-attention layers with positional encoding. What could the possible issues be? 1. Can you design a learnable positional encoding method? 1. Can we assign different learned embeddings according to different offsets between queries and keys that are compared in self-attention? Hint: you may refer to relative position embeddings . :begin_tab:mxnet Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:pytorch Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:tensorflow Discussions :end_tab: :begin_tab:jax Discussions :end_tab:

sources.title: Dive into Deep Learning — 4.1 Softmax Regression
sources.author: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, and Alexander J. Smola
sources.text: # Softmax Regression In , we introduced linear regression, working through implementations from scratch in and again using high-level APIs of a deep learning framework in to do the heavy lifting. Regression is the hammer we reach for when we want to answer how much? or how many? questions. If you want to predict the number of dollars (price) at which a house will be sold, or the number of wins a baseball team might have, or the number of days that a patient will remain hospitalized before being discharged, then you are probably looking for a regression model. However, even within regression models, there are important distinctions. For instance, the price of a house will never be negative and changes might often be relative to its baseline price. As such, it might be more effective to regress on the logarithm of the price. Likewise, the number of days a patient spends in hospital is a discrete nonnegative random variable. As such, least mean squares might not be an ideal approach either. This sort of time-to-event modeling comes with a host of other complications that are dealt with in a specialized subfield called survival modeling. The point here is not to overwhelm you but just to let you know that there is a lot more to estimation than simply minimizing squared errors. And more broadly, there is a lot more to supervised learning than regression. In this section, we focus on classification problems where we put aside how much? questions and instead focus on which category? questions. Does this email belong in the spam folder or the inbox? Is this customer more likely to sign up or not to sign up for a subscription service? Does this image depict a donkey, a dog, a cat, or a rooster? Which movie is Aston most likely to watch next? Which section of the book are you going to read next? Colloquially, machine learning practitioners overload the word classification to describe two subtly different problems: (i) those where we are interested only in hard assignments of examples to categories (classes); and (ii) those where we wish to make soft assignments, i.e., to assess the probability that each category applies. The distinction tends to get blurred, in part, because often, even when we only care about hard assignments, we still use models that make soft assignments. Even more, there are cases where more than one label might be true. For instance, a news article might simultaneously cover the topics of entertainment, business, and space flight, but not the topics of medicine or sports. Thus, categorizing it into one of the above categories on their own would not be very useful. This problem is commonly known as multi-label classification. See for an overview and for an effective algorithm when tagging images. ## Classification To get our feet wet, let's start with a simple image classification problem. Here, each input consists of a grayscale image. We can represent each pixel value with a single scalar, giving us four features . Further, let's assume that each image belongs to one among the categories "cat", "chicken", and "dog". Next, we have to choose how to represent the labels. We have two obvious choices. Perhaps the most natural impulse would be to choose , where the integers represent respectively. This is a great way of storing such information on a computer. If the categories had some natural ordering among them, say if we were trying to predict , then it might even make sense to cast this as an ordinal regression problem and keep the labels in this format. See for an overview of different types of ranking loss functions and for a Bayesian approach that addresses responses with more than one mode. In general, classification problems do not come with natural orderings among the classes. Fortunately, statisticians long ago invented a simple way to represent categorical data: the one-hot encoding. A one-hot encoding is a vector with as many components as we have categories. The component corresponding to a particular instance's category is set to 1 and all other components are set to 0. In our case, a label y would be a three-dimensional vector, with corresponding to "cat", to "chicken", and to "dog": ### Linear Model In order to estimate the conditional probabilities associated with all the possible classes, we need a model with multiple outputs, one per class. To address classification with linear models, we will need as many affine functions as we have outputs. Strictly speaking, we only need one fewer, since the final category has to be the difference between 1 and the sum of the other categories, but for reasons of symmetry we use a slightly redundant parametrization. Each output corresponds to its own affine function. In our case, since we have 4 features and 3 possible output categories, we need 12 scalars to represent the weights (w with subscripts), and 3 scalars to represent the biases (b with subscripts). This yields: The corresponding neural network diagram is shown in . Just as in linear regression, we use a single-layer neural network. And since the calculation of each output, , and , depends on every input, , , , and , the output layer can also be described as a fully connected layer. Softmax regression is a single-layer neural network. For a more concise notation we use vectors and matrices: is much better suited for mathematics and code. Note that we have gathered all of our weights into a matrix and all biases in a vector. ### The Softmax Assuming a suitable loss function, we could try, directly, to minimize the difference between and the labels . While it turns out that treating classification as a vector-valued regression problem works surprisingly well, it is nonetheless unsatisfactory in the following ways: There is no guarantee that the outputs sum up to 1 in the way we expect probabilities to behave. There is no guarantee that the outputs are even nonnegative, even if their outputs sum up to 1, or that they do not exceed 1. Both aspects render the estimation problem difficult to solve and the solution very brittle to outliers. For instance, if we assume that there is a positive linear dependency between the number of bedrooms and the likelihood that someone will buy a house, the probability might exceed 1 when it comes to buying a mansion! As such, we need a mechanism to "squish" the outputs. There are many ways we might accomplish this goal. For instance, we could assume that the outputs are corrupted versions of , where the corruption occurs by means of adding noise drawn from a normal distribution. In other words, , where . This is the so-called probit model, first introduced by . While appealing, it does not work quite as well nor lead to a particularly nice optimization problem, when compared to the softmax. Another way to accomplish this goal (and to ensure nonnegativity) is to use an exponential function . This does indeed satisfy the requirement that the conditional class probability increases with increasing , it is monotonic, and all probabilities are nonnegative. We can then transform these values so that they add up to 1 by dividing each by their sum. This process is called normalization. Putting these two pieces together gives us the softmax function: Note that the largest coordinate of corresponds to the most likely class according to . Moreover, because the softmax operation preserves the ordering among its arguments, we do not need to compute the softmax to determine which class has been assigned the highest probability. Thus, The idea of a softmax dates back to , who adapted ideas from physics. Dating even further back, Boltzmann, the father of modern statistical physics, used this trick to model a distribution over energy states in gas molecules. In particular, he discovered that the prevalence of a state of energy in a thermodynamic ensemble, such as the molecules in a gas, is proportional to . Here, E is the energy of a state, T is the temperature, and k is the Boltzmann constant. When statisticians talk about increasing or decreasing the "temperature" of a statistical system, they refer to changing T in order to favor lower or higher energy states. Following Gibbs' idea, energy equates to error. Energy-based models use this point of view when describing problems in deep learning. ### Vectorization To improve computational efficiency, we vectorize calculations in minibatches of data. Assume that we are given a minibatch of n examples with dimensionality (number of inputs) d. Moreover, assume that we have q categories in the output. Then the weights satisfy and the bias satisfies . This accelerates the dominant operation into a matrix--matrix product . Moreover, since each row in represents a data example, the softmax operation itself can be computed rowwise: for each row of , exponentiate all entries and then normalize them by the sum. Note, though, that care must be taken to avoid exponentiating and taking logarithms of large numbers, since this can cause numerical overflow or underflow. Deep learning frameworks take care of this automatically. ## Loss Function Now that we have a mapping from features to probabilities , we need a way to optimize the accuracy of this mapping. We will rely on maximum likelihood estimation, the very same method that we encountered when providing a probabilistic justification for the mean squared error loss in . ### Log-Likelihood The softmax function gives us a vector , which we can interpret as the (estimated) conditional probabilities of each class, given any input , such as = . In the following we assume that for a dataset with features the labels are represented using a one-hot encoding label vector. We can compare the estimates with reality by checking how probable the actual classes are according to our model, given the features: We are allowed to use the factorization since we assume that each label is drawn independently from its respective distribution . Since maximizing the product of terms is awkward, we take the negative logarithm to obtain the equivalent problem of minimizing the negative log-likelihood: where for any pair of label and model prediction over q classes, the loss function l is For reasons explained later on, the loss function in is commonly called the cross-entropy loss. Since is a one-hot vector of length q, the sum over all its coordinates j vanishes for all but one term. Note that the loss is bounded from below by 0 whenever is a probability vector: no single entry is larger than 1, hence their negative logarithm cannot be lower than 0; only if we predict the actual label with certainty. This can never happen for any finite setting of the weights because taking a softmax output towards 1 requires taking the corresponding input to infinity (or all other outputs for to negative infinity). Even if our model could assign an output probability of 0, any error made when assigning such high confidence would incur infinite loss ( ). ### Softmax and Cross-Entropy Loss Since the softmax function and the corresponding cross-entropy loss are so common, it is worth understanding a bit better how they are computed. Plugging into the definition of the loss in and using the definition of the softmax we obtain To understand a bit better what is going on, consider the derivative with respect to any logit . We get In other words, the derivative is the difference between the probability assigned by our model, as expressed by the softmax operation, and what actually happened, as expressed by elements in the one-hot label vector. In this sense, it is very similar to what we saw in regression, where the gradient was the difference between the observation y and estimate . This is not a coincidence.
